title: Te Hauora o te Awa: The Health of Our River / Te Hauora o te Awa: Te Oranga o te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This lesson is centered around Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway that flows through our rohe. According to Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau, this awa was named after the kōtukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once lined its banks, providing medicinal resources and habitat for native species. Students will explore how the health of our awa directly connects to the physical, mental, social, and spiritual wellbeing of our community, drawing on traditional knowledge of water as a taonga and life source.
learningObjectives: Analyze how the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku impacts the holistic wellbeing (hauora) of our local community using the Whare Tapa Whā model
Apply mātauranga Māori principles to assess current environmental challenges facing our awa and propose culturally-grounded solutions
Collaborate with local kaumātua and environmental experts to collect water quality data and interpret findings through both scientific and cultural lenses
Design and implement a community action plan that addresses an identified health issue related to our awa

activities.title: Whakapapa o te Awa: Connecting to Our Waterway
activities.description: Students begin with a karakia at the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, listening to local kaumātua share pūrākau (traditional stories) about the awa's significance. They create visual maps tracing the connection between the health of the waterway and the four dimensions of hauora (taha tinana, taha hinengaro, taha whānau, taha wairua).

activities.title: Mātaki, Rongo, Rongoā: Observing, Sensing, and Healing
activities.description: Using traditional observation methods (mātaki) and sensory awareness (rongo), students assess the current state of the awa. They learn about traditional rongoā (medicinal) plants that once grew along the banks and investigate how environmental changes have affected these resources and community health.

activities.title: He Tohu Aroha: Scientific and Cultural Water Testing
activities.description: Students work with local environmental scientists and cultural experts to conduct water quality testing, comparing scientific measurements (pH, nitrate levels) with cultural indicators (clarity, presence of native species, cultural health). They document both data sets and analyze correlations.

activities.title: Whare Tapa Whā o te Awa: Holistic Analysis
activities.description: Working in groups, students apply the Whare Tapa Whā model to create a comprehensive assessment of how the awa's health affects community wellbeing. Each group focuses on one dimension of hauora and presents findings to the class, highlighting interconnections between the dimensions.

activities.title: Manaaki te Awa: Community Action Project
activities.description: Students design and implement a practical action to improve the health of the awa and community wellbeing. Options include planting native vegetation, creating educational resources for the community, organizing a stream clean-up event, or developing proposals for local council consideration.

resources: Local kaumātua from Ngāti Kahungunu with knowledge of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water quality testing kits and data on Mangakōtukutuku Stream
'Te Mātauranga o ngā Awa' resource from Te Papa Tongarewa
Traditional Māori healing plants guide from local marae experts

tasks: Create a comprehensive report analyzing the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using both scientific data and cultural indicators
Develop and present a community action proposal that addresses an identified issue affecting the awa and community wellbeing
Participate in a group reflection discussing how the health of the awa connects to personal and community hauora

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A1 - Personal health and physical development
Health and Physical Education: C1 - Relationships with other people
Health and Physical Education: D1 - Healthy communities and environments
Science: Living World: Life processes, ecology, and evolution
Social Sciences: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
